failures.html: On the 20th anniversary of the Agile Manifesto, we ask whether the adoption of agile thinking and practices has improved business operations?
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: 2021 marked the 20th anniversary of the Agile Manifesto, created when 17 software experts met in Snowbird, Utah, to discuss how to radically improve the delivery of software products.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Since that time, the Agile movement has grown to be a global phenomenon, not only in the software field, but across all aspects of business and government. And understandably so – what business or government agency would not want to be seen as being agile?
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: So, how have we done? Has the adoption of agile thinking and practices improved the operation of government and businesses? Or has it just been another fad, like Lean, Six Sigma and TQM, that will also fade into obscurity?
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Let’s assess the success (or otherwise) of the Agile movement by revisiting the four underlying values of Agile Manifesto:
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html:
• Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
  • Working software over comprehensive documentation
  • Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
  • Responding to change over following a plan
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: On the positive side: organisations that have truly embraced a “One Touch Deployment” approach are able to release tested, production-ready software literally at the press of a button. This is an amazing improvement over what happened pre-2000.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: On the negative side: unfortunately, the number of organisations that can do “One Touch Deployment” is very few – the majority of organisations still take an inordinate amount of time and money to get new product features developed and shipped.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: While there have been valiant attempts to improve collaboration in product and service development, this has been more effective inside the organisation than between them. Contracts between organisations have proven to be a stumbling block to agility.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Collaboration requires common goals, a high degree of trust, openness and transparency. Contracts, on the other hand, remain adversarial in the main – dividing up the pie rather than growing it.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Responding to change over following a plan
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: This is where I think we have probably seen the most improvement from the adoption of Agile thinking generally.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Also, while time and budget are both critical elements of any meaningful endeavour, they are increasingly seen as the constraints that they actually are, rather than fixed objectives to be met in their own right.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: This is my subjective take on the success of the Agile movement on its 20th anniversary – your mileage may vary. But I do firmly believe that in the rush to adopt Agile, organisations are still leaving a lot on the table.

failures.html: On the 20th anniversary of the Agile Manifesto, we ask whether the adoption of agile thinking and practices has improved business operations?
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: 2021 marked the 20th anniversary of the Agile Manifesto, created when 17 software experts met in Snowbird, Utah, to discuss how to radically improve the delivery of software products.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Since that time, the Agile movement has grown to be a global phenomenon, not only in the software field, but across all aspects of business and government. And understandably so – what business or government agency would not want to be seen as being agile?
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: So, how have we done? Has the adoption of agile thinking and practices improved the operation of government and businesses? Or has it just been another fad, like Lean, Six Sigma and TQM, that will also fade into obscurity?
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Let’s assess the success (or otherwise) of the Agile movement by revisiting the four underlying values of Agile Manifesto:
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html:
• Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
  • Working software over comprehensive documentation
  • Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
  • Responding to change over following a plan
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: On the positive side: organisations that have truly embraced a “One Touch Deployment” approach are able to release tested, production-ready software literally at the press of a button. This is an amazing improvement over what happened pre-2000.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: On the negative side: unfortunately, the number of organisations that can do “One Touch Deployment” is very few – the majority of organisations still take an inordinate amount of time and money to get new product features developed and shipped.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: While there have been valiant attempts to improve collaboration in product and service development, this has been more effective inside the organisation than between them. Contracts between organisations have proven to be a stumbling block to agility.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Collaboration requires common goals, a high degree of trust, openness and transparency. Contracts, on the other hand, remain adversarial in the main – dividing up the pie rather than growing it.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Responding to change over following a plan
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: This is where I think we have probably seen the most improvement from the adoption of Agile thinking generally.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: Also, while time and budget are both critical elements of any meaningful endeavour, they are increasingly seen as the constraints that they actually are, rather than fixed objectives to be met in their own right.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
All page content should be contained by landmarks

failures.html: This is my subjective take on the success of the Agile movement on its 20th anniversary – your mileage may vary. But I do firmly believe that in the rush to adopt Agile, organisations are still leaving a lot on the table.
failures.message: All page content should be contained by landmarks
